content.text: Operational readiness is the set of conditions that must be satisfied before an agent capability is ready for production operation by an on-call team that was not involved in building it. A capability that is technically correct and safely validated is not ready for production until someone who did not build it can operate, debug, and if necessary disable it quickly. The DoD's operational readiness section ensures this handoff happens as part of the delivery process, not as an afterthought discovered during the first 2 AM incident.
What it is

Operational readiness in the Definition of Done is a set of completion criteria that verify a new agent capability has been instrumented, documented, and validated to the degree required for on-call engineers to operate it in production. It covers four areas: monitoring (dashboards and alerting configured and validated), runbook (failure diagnosis and recovery procedures documented and reviewed by SRE), rollback (a tested procedure for disabling or reverting the capability), and on-call handoff (the engineering team responsible for the capability has briefed the on-call rotation and updated paging rules).

Operational readiness criteria are distinct from functional correctness (the capability does what it is supposed to do) and safety validation (the capability has been evaluated for safety risks). A capability can be functionally correct and safety-validated but still operationally unready if there is no runbook, no dashboard, or no rollback procedure. All three dimensions must be satisfied before the capability is considered truly done and safe to deploy.
Why it matters

The most common pattern in agent production incidents is: a capability ships, works well for days or weeks, then encounters an unexpected input pattern or dependency failure. The on-call engineer is paged at an inconvenient time, opens the system to debug it, and finds no dashboard, no runbook, and no clear way to disable the capability without redeploying the entire application. They spend 45 minutes reconstructing context that the building engineer could have provided in a 2-page runbook written in 30 minutes before deployment.

Operational readiness requirements shift this work from the incident response phase to the delivery phase. This is almost always more efficient: the building engineer has deep context about the capability's design and failure modes, can write accurate runbooks while the implementation is fresh, and can test the rollback procedure against a staging environment rather than under production pressure. Every minute invested in operational readiness before deployment saves multiples of that time during incidents.
Benefits
Faster incident resolution: runbooks and dashboards created before deployment enable on-call engineers to diagnose and resolve issues in minutes rather than hours.
Reduced incident frequency: monitoring configured before deployment catches issues that would otherwise go undetected until a customer reports them.
Knowledge transfer: operational readiness requirements force the building team to externalize capability knowledge in a form that any competent engineer can use.
Rollback confidence: a tested rollback procedure means the team can reduce impact during incidents immediately rather than spending time figuring out how to disable a capability under pressure.
Cost control: capacity planning and cost circuit breakers configured before deployment prevent unexpected cost overruns from becoming a production incident.
Accountability: SRE sign-off on the runbook creates a formal handoff moment where the SRE team acknowledges they can operate the capability, rather than inheriting it without explicit acceptance.
Trade-offs and limitations
Runbook maintenance: runbooks written at deployment time become stale as the capability evolves; they must be updated with each significant change, adding ongoing maintenance overhead.
SRE review bottleneck: if SRE sign-off is required for every capability, the SRE team's review capacity limits deployment throughput; this must be managed with SLA agreements.
Dashboard proliferation: each new capability adding new dashboards without a coherent dashboard strategy creates noise and makes the overall monitoring system harder to navigate.

Staged rollback complexity: for capabilities deeply integrated into the application flow, rollback may not be simple feature-flag toggle; the rollback procedure may be complex and hard to test thoroughly in staging.
Operational readiness theater: teams under delivery pressure may create minimal runbooks and dashboards that technically satisfy the checklist without providing genuine operational value.
How it works

The operational readiness DoD criteria are evaluated in the staging environment before production deployment is requested. The building team creates dashboards in the team's observability platform (Grafana, Datadog, CloudWatch) that show the capability's request rate, error rate, latency percentiles, and per-request cost. They configure alert rules that trigger when error rate exceeds 1% for 5 minutes, or latency p95 exceeds the SLA threshold, or cost per request exceeds 2x the estimated baseline. They test that alerts fire correctly by triggering synthetic failures in staging.

The runbook is written to answer the questions an on-call engineer would ask during an incident: what does this capability do, what are its dependencies, how do I know if it is misbehaving, what are the most common failure modes and their remediation steps, and how do I disable it if necessary. The runbook is reviewed by an SRE team member who was not involved in building the capability; their review confirms that the runbook is understandable by a non-expert and that the described procedures can be executed without additional context from the building team.

The rollback procedure is documented as a specific sequence of steps (feature flag off, deployment revert command, or both) and executed in the staging environment to verify it works end-to-end. The expected time to rollback is measured and documented; if it exceeds the team's recovery time target, the capability design or deployment approach must be revised to achieve a faster rollback path before production deployment is approved.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Operational readiness DoD showing four areas: monitoring, runbook, rollback, on-call handoff, plus staging validation and SRE sign-off

content.text: The diagram shows the four required operational readiness areas and the two gate steps (staging validation and SRE sign-off) that must complete before production deployment. The bottom row shows the additional capacity planning requirements: token budget times RPS gives an estimated monthly cost, and a cost circuit breaker is configured to catch unexpected cost growth automatically.
When to use it

Full operational readiness requirements apply to all agent capabilities deployed to production environments that handle real user traffic. Lighter requirements (monitoring only, no SRE sign-off) may apply to internal-only capabilities or capabilities deployed to isolated sandbox environments. The full requirements also apply to existing capabilities being significantly modified: a significant change to an agent's behavior or architecture requires updating the runbook, dashboard, and rollback procedure to reflect the new design.

When a capability is being deployed incrementally (canary to 1% of traffic, then 10%, then 100%), operational readiness is required before the initial canary deployment, not just before full rollout. The entire traffic exposure period is production exposure; incidents at 1% traffic are still production incidents, and the on-call team needs the same operational tools at 1% that they would need at 100%.
How to implement
Define the minimum dashboard content in a standard template: capability name, request rate, error rate, latency p50/p95/p99, cost per request, and a link to the runbook.
Create a runbook template with required sections: capability description, dependencies, SLO targets, common failure modes with remediation steps, and disable procedure.
Establish the SRE review process: define who on the SRE team reviews runbooks, how they are notified, and what their maximum review SLA is.
Implement the cost circuit breaker pattern: a per-capability cost budget configured in the observability platform that alerts and optionally disables the capability if exceeded.
Define the rollback procedure template: standard steps for feature-flag disable and, for capabilities without feature flags, the deployment revert procedure.
Add operational readiness items to the DoD checklist with links to the specific dashboard, alert rules, and runbook that have been created for the capability.
Require staging validation for every new capability: the capability must have operated in staging for at least 24 hours with realistic traffic before production deployment is approved.

Conduct a post-deployment operational readiness review after the first 30 days: check whether the dashboards and alerts are providing useful signal, update the runbook based on any early operational experience, and refine alert thresholds.
Examples

# Runbook: Customer Support Agent — Document Retrieval Capability
**Last updated:** 2025-07-12  
**Owner:** @ai-platform-team  
**On-call escalation:** @platform-oncall → @ai-lead

## What this capability does
Retrieves relevant support documents from the knowledge base using semantic search
and provides them to the customer support agent as context for answering queries.

## Dependencies
- Knowledge base API: `kb.internal.example.com` (P1 dependency — capability fails if unavailable)
- Embedding model: `text-embedding-3-small` via AI gateway
- Cache: Redis cluster `kb-cache-prod` (optional — degrades gracefully if unavailable)

## SLO targets
- Availability: 99.9% (measured as successful document retrievals / total requests)
- Latency p95: < 800ms
- Cost per request: < $0.002

## Common failure modes

### Knowledge base API timeout
**Symptoms:** Error rate spike on `kb_retrieval_timeout_total` metric  
**Remediation:** Check KB API health at `https://status.internal.example.com/kb`. 
If KB API is degraded, disable retrieval (see Disable Procedure) — agent falls back to general knowledge.

### Embedding latency increase
**Symptoms:** p95 latency > 1500ms; cost per request increase  
**Remediation:** Check AI gateway queue depth. If queue depth > 1000, reduce request rate
via feature flag `kb_retrieval_concurrency_limit`.

### Cache miss storm (Redis unavailable)
**Symptoms:** Error rate stable, latency spike, cost increase (more embedding calls)  
**Remediation:** Redis degraded mode is automatic. Monitor cost circuit breaker alert.
If cost exceeds 2x baseline for > 10 minutes, disable retrieval (see below).

## Disable Procedure
1. Open LaunchDarkly, search for flag `customer_support_kb_retrieval`
2. Set flag to OFF for all users (estimated rollback time: 30 seconds)
3. Verify error rate returns to baseline in Grafana dashboard
4. Page @ai-lead if retrieval disable causes agent quality degradation reports
Real-world example

A retail company deployed an agent for product recommendations. The feature shipped quickly — the code was correct, it passed all tests, and the first two weeks in production were incident-free. On a Wednesday evening, the recommendation agent began returning empty result sets due to a dependency API rate limit that the team had not anticipated. The on-call engineer was paged, found no dashboard for the recommendation agent, and spent 40 minutes determining that the issue was API rate limiting rather than a code bug. There was no feature flag — disabling the agent required a code deployment, which took 20 additional minutes to prepare and deploy.

The team spent the next sprint building the operational infrastructure that should have been present from day one: a Grafana dashboard, alert rules, a runbook documenting the API rate limit as a known failure mode, and a feature flag. They implemented an operational readiness DoD requirement that prevented future capabilities from shipping without this infrastructure. Six months later, when a similar API rate limit issue occurred with a different agent capability, the on-call engineer resolved it in 8 minutes by following the runbook and toggling the feature flag.
Common mistakes
Writing runbooks that only the building team can understand by using internal jargon, unexplained acronyms, or references to context that an on-call engineer would not have.
Creating dashboards that display every available metric rather than the three or four metrics that indicate whether the capability is working correctly.
Testing rollback in development but not in staging, discovering during a production incident that the rollback procedure does not work in the production environment.
Skipping SRE review when delivery timelines are tight, treating it as optional rather than mandatory — the capability then ships without independent verification that it is operable.
Defining SLOs without discussing them with the business; an SLO set by engineering without product input may not reflect actual user tolerance for latency or errors.
Not updating the runbook when the capability changes, causing on-call engineers to follow outdated remediation steps during incidents.
Best practices
Write runbooks assuming zero prior knowledge of the capability; they should be actionable for a competent engineer who has never seen this system before.
Test that alerts actually fire by injecting synthetic failures in staging; alert rules that have never been triggered may be misconfigured.
Include a cost circuit breaker for every agent capability: configure an alert that fires if cost-per-request exceeds 2x the estimated baseline for more than 10 minutes.
Practice rollback procedures quarterly: as the application evolves, rollback procedures can break silently; scheduled practice keeps them current.
Co-author runbooks with SRE: involve an SRE engineer in the runbook writing process, not just the final review, to get operational perspective embedded in the runbook from the start.
Set SLOs based on user impact data: use production error rate and latency data from similar capabilities to calibrate what threshold represents a user-impacting degradation.
Performance and scalability

Operational readiness work scales sub-linearly with capability count when teams invest in shared infrastructure. A standard dashboard template, a runbook generator that pre-populates standard sections from capability metadata, and a feature flag management system with self-service provisioning reduce the per-capability operational readiness effort significantly. At 5 capabilities, every operational readiness item is built from scratch; at 50 capabilities, the team should be reusing 70% of the infrastructure and only building the capability-specific sections.

SRE review capacity is the most common bottleneck at scale. Managing this requires: a tiered review process (lightweight self-service checklist for low-risk capabilities, full SRE review for high-risk ones), a maximum review SLA that prevents the SRE team from becoming a deployment blocker, and investment in self-service operational templates that reduce the depth of review required per capability.

Security and reliability

Operational readiness items have direct security implications. A feature flag is not just a rollback mechanism — it is an emergency circuit breaker that can disable a misbehaving agent capability before it causes further harm. The ability to toggle a capability off within seconds, without a code deployment, is a critical incident response capability for agent systems that are actively misused or producing harmful outputs. The DoD requirement to have this mechanism ready and tested before production deployment is a security as well as operational control.

For reliability, the combination of pre-configured alerts and a tested runbook with a known rollback procedure enables teams to achieve mean time to recovery (MTTR) targets that are impossible without operational readiness. An alert that fires within 2 minutes of an incident, a runbook that guides the on-call engineer to the right diagnosis in 3 minutes, and a rollback that executes in 30 seconds can reduce MTTR from 60+ minutes to under 10 minutes. This improvement directly reduces the customer impact of agent capability incidents.
Additional implementation considerations

Implementing these practices effectively requires understanding both the technical and organizational context in which they will operate. Teams at different stages of AI maturity will apply these practices differently: a team that has operated AI systems in production for two years has a richer base of operational experience to inform their implementation choices than a team deploying their first agent. Neither team should simply adopt a template; both should evaluate each practice against their specific risk profile, operational constraints, and team capabilities.

Cross-functional collaboration is essential for sustainable implementation. Engineering, AI safety, product, and SRE teams all have legitimate interests in how these practices are designed and enforced. Practices designed without input from the teams who must live with them tend to be either too abstract to apply consistently or too prescriptive to accommodate the real variability in situations they will encounter. Invest in the collaborative design process upfront; the time spent building shared understanding saves time in adoption and reduces the resistance that comes from practices imposed without consultation.

Measurement is the foundation of continuous improvement. Each practice described here has an associated metric: review cycle time, DoD completion rate, eval pass rate, incident rate, checklist failure distribution. Teams that track these metrics and review them regularly can identify whether their implementations are achieving their intended effects, where friction is causing workarounds, and where investment in tooling or capacity would provide the highest return. Practices that are not measured are not improved; they either remain static while the environment changes around them or gradually decay as their original purpose is forgotten.

Finally, recognize that the practices described here are means, not ends. The ends are: agent capabilities that work reliably, behave safely, can be operated and maintained by the team, and create genuine value for users. Every practice should be evaluated against these outcomes. When a practice is creating overhead without demonstrably contributing to these outcomes, it should be reformed or replaced. When a practice is clearly preventing incidents or improving quality, it should be maintained even when it creates friction. The discipline of outcome-oriented engineering process management is what separates teams that maintain effective practices long-term from teams whose practices gradually become compliance exercises.
Capacity planning as operational readiness

Capacity planning is part of operational readiness because an agent capability that exceeds its token budget or cost envelope in production creates a financial incident in addition to a technical one. Before deploying a capability to production, the team estimates the expected request volume, the average tokens per request, and the resulting monthly cost. This estimate is reviewed by the product manager or cost owner who approves the budget, and a cost circuit breaker is configured in the observability platform to alert if the actual cost exceeds 2x the estimate for a sustained period.

For agent capabilities that may have highly variable token usage (such as capabilities that process documents of varying length), capacity planning should include a worst-case analysis based on the P99 token count, not just the average. A capability that averages 1,000 tokens per request but has a P99 of 15,000 tokens (due to long documents) will have a very different cost profile under production traffic than the average-based estimate suggests. Token budget enforcement at the application level (truncating inputs that exceed the per-request budget) prevents individual requests from causing cost spikes while the capacity planning establishes the expected baseline.

The cost estimate and the circuit breaker threshold should be linked in the DoD checklist so that the tech lead and PM can verify both during the DoD review. A capability that has an approved cost estimate but no circuit breaker is operationally incomplete; the circuit breaker is the mechanism that enforces the estimate in production rather than just tracking it after the fact. Without the circuit breaker, a cost overrun may not be detected until the monthly billing cycle, by which point the accumulated overrun may be significant.
Incident post-mortems and operational readiness updates

Every production incident related to an agent capability should trigger an operational readiness review: examine what information was missing during the incident that would have been present if operational readiness had been fully implemented, and add what was missing to the runbook, dashboard, or alert configuration. Post-mortems that only address the root cause of the immediate issue without updating operational documentation are incomplete; they fix today's incident but leave tomorrow's harder.

A specific post-mortem action item format for operational readiness: 'During the incident, on-call spent X minutes determining Y because Z was missing from the runbook / dashboard / alert configuration. Action: add Z before the next deployment of this capability.' This format makes the link between incident experience and operational readiness improvement explicit, and creates a trackable action item that can be included in the next sprint.
Key takeaways
Operational readiness is a mandatory DoD requirement, not a nice-to-have: a capability that cannot be operated safely by the on-call team is not ready for production deployment.
Four areas must be covered: monitoring (dashboard + alerts), runbook (diagnosis + recovery), rollback (tested procedure + feature flag), and on-call handoff (briefing + paging rules).
Runbooks must be written for an engineer with zero prior knowledge of the capability; internal jargon and assumed context make runbooks useless during high-pressure incidents.
SRE review of the runbook before deployment is a quality gate, not a bureaucratic step; it verifies that the runbook is genuinely operational without knowledge of the building team.
Cost circuit breakers must be configured for every agent capability before production deployment; unexpected cost growth from agent capabilities can be large and fast.
Invest in shared operational infrastructure (dashboard templates, runbook generators, standard feature flag patterns) to reduce the per-capability cost of operational readiness over time.
